result:
**The River That Carries Us: A Meditation on Time, Memory, and the Shape of Days**

There is a river that runs behind the house where I grew up, though I hesitate to call it a river—more a restless, muddy vein that swells with spring rain and shrinks to cracked earth by August. We called it “the crick,” a word that tasted like childhood itself, all elbows and scraped knees. I have not seen it in two decades, yet it visits me at odd hours: when I’m stirring coffee and the spoon clinks the rim of the cup, when I’m waiting for a stoplight to change, when I’m supposed to be listening to someone tell me about their vacation. Suddenly I’m eight again, crouched on its banks, watching a crawdad back out of a tin can like a retiring shopkeeper pulling down his shutters. The water was never clear; it carried the runoff of soybean fields, the occasional bloom of algae, the ghosted reflection of a red-winged blackbird. But to me it was the Atlantic. I spent whole summers trying to dam it with rocks and scraps of plywood, convinced that if I could just make the water pause, it would reveal something—treasure, dinosaur bones, the answer to why my father’s voice carried the weight of iron at night. The river always breached my barricades by morning, and I would begin again, Sisyphus in cutoff shorts.

Memory works like that: it obeys no hydraulics of scale. A drip in a basement can sound like a cathedral bell at 3 a.m. The smell of crayons can unpack an entire classroom—Mrs. Albright’s third-grade, the way she used to hum “Puff the Magic Dragon” while handing out worksheets, the time Jeremy wet his pants during the fire drill and tried to blame the spreading stain on the water fountain. We think of memory as a filing cabinet, but it’s more like a Tibetan sky burial: pieces carted off by whatever scavenger emotion happens to be circling. Some days you’re left with just a collarbone of the original moment, some days the whole carcass drops.

I’ve been thinking about time lately, how it used to be a hallway lined with doors—first grade, summer camp, learner’s permit, dorm keys, first apartment. You walked through and the door locked behind you. But sometime around thirty the metaphor collapsed; now it’s less a hallway than a pinball machine, and I’m the ball. I ricochet off bumpers labeled “your mother’s new hips,” “the price of strawberries,” “your ex’s wedding announcement,” flashing lights and bells, no clue where the drain is. The only certainty is motion, a humming that sometimes feels like flight and sometimes like falling, depending on whether I’ve had coffee.

The physicists tell us time doesn’t flow; it just is. Past, present, future are stubborn illusions born of thermodynamics and the way our brains are wired to sort cause from effect. I read this in a library book—its due date stamped in violent red on the slip inside the back cover, a little analog gravestone. The book claimed that every moment persists somewhere, like frames of film stacked in a cosmic projector. If so, then eight-year-old me is forever pinching a leech off his ankle, the creature stretching like bubble gum before snapping back to its original slug-shape. My grandmother is eternally lifting her oxygen mask to ask if I want a root beer float, even though she died in a year when the cicadas were so loud we had to close the casket quickly lest they mistake the funeral for a mating call. And somewhere, presumably, I am already dying—though in which direction, the book refused to say.

I used to keep a journal. Spiral notebooks filled between the ages of thirteen and twenty-four, stored now in a box my mother keeps threatening to “donate to the Goodwill.” The early ones are urgent with capital letters: “I WILL NEVER SPEAK TO MOM AGAIN” because she wouldn’t let me get my eyebrow pierced. Later entries grow coy, abbreviating boys to single letters—“K. kissed me behind the band room, tasted like chlorine.” Reading them is like watching someone build a stranger out of Lego: here the hair snapped on, here the opinions about god, here the sudden left turn into anorexia the fall sophomore year. I kept track of my weight in the margins, numbers marching downward like troops retreating from a front they never meant to defend. The last entry is from a Greyhound bus somewhere in Kansas at 4 a.m. I’d just broken up with a woman who introduced me to cumin and the concept that one could be allergic to wool. I wrote: “Cornfields in the dark look like an ocean that’s been convinced to hold still.” Then I stopped. Not for any dramatic reason; the pen just ran out of ink, and I never bought another. Sometimes I think the rest of my life has been an attempt to finish that sentence.

We are, all of us, unreliable narrators of our own stories. The brain abhors a vacuum; it fills gaps with spackle made of whatever’s handy—old movies, dreams, things that happened to cousins. I have a vivid memory of breaking my arm on a trampoline in second grade, the bone protruding like a snapped breadstick. Except my mother swears I never broke a bone, that I was never even on a trampoline as a child. So where did the image come from? A public-service announcement? A neighbor’s story grafted onto my skin? The arm in question aches before storms, phantom evidence. Maybe memory is just another word for the stories we agree to carry, the way river stones grow smooth not from a single season but from the patience of water insisting, “This shape is possible.”

When my daughter was born, time did something new: it folded. Here was this creature with my grandmother’s widow’s peak, my mother’s skepticism in the way her newborn brow furrowed, as if to say, “I’ve seen this trick before.” Holding her at 3 a.m. while the radiator clanked out Morse code, I felt the past and future braid. I remembered my father walking me in circles to the soundtrack of “Blackbird” played on a cassette whose ribbon kept jamming. Now I was the one whispering, “You are loved, you are loved,” though the phrase felt too small for the enormity of what I meant. I wanted to say: “You are the latest draft of a story written by cave painters, plague survivors, enslaved people who sang in languages that no longer exist, women who pretended to be stupid so their husbands wouldn’t steal the money they earned sewing quilts.” Instead I said, “Let’s try the football hold; maybe she’ll burp,” because exhaustion turns even miracles into logistics.

She’s seven now, and recently asked if we could build a time machine out of a cardboard box. We painted it silver, using a roll of aluminum foil my grandmother had bought in 1997 and never opened. The plan was to travel to the Cretaceous, but we got distracted by yesterday instead—she wanted to see what she looked like as a baby. We found a video of her learning to walk: the uncertain gravity, the drunk delight of each step. Watching her watch herself, I realized memory starts out as something we inherit from others—photographs, stories, the way my mother sings “Moon River” off-key every New Year’s Eve. Only gradually do we become the authors, scribbling in margins, crossing out. By the time you’re old enough to wonder who you are, the text is already palimpsest, a thousand fingerprints on the same mirror.

People always say children grow up fast, but no one mentions that parents do, too. One day you’re cutting grapes in half to prevent choking; the next you’re being asked for a phone and having to Google whether “finsta” is a drug or a filter. The river doesn’t care that you once built a fortress of sticks; it’s busy moving on, eroding the bank where you carved initials inside a heart that included both you and a girl who later joined the Navy and married her recruiter. The trick, I suppose, is to love the erosion, to stand waist-deep in the current and feel the sand shift underfoot without clutching at it. Though I’ve never been good at that. I still have concert tickets from 1998 in a drawer, the ink faded to hieroglyphics. I keep them like tiny lifeboats, though the band broke up, the venue turned into a Walmart, and the girl I went with is somewhere in Oregon teaching Pilates to dogs—yes, that’s a real job, she keeps posting videos of golden retrievers holding downward dog.

The pandemic did something strange to time: it accordion-pleated. Some months stretched like taffy; others collapsed into single days that tasted like metal. I learned the names of birds I’d never noticed—tufted titmouse, Carolina wren—because their songs were suddenly audible without traffic. I watched a neighbor’s cherry tree bloom, drop petals, fruit, then stand bare, all while I was still on the same Zoom call. My daughter built fairy houses out of sticks and left them on the sidewalk; strangers added tiny pebble pathways, bottle-cap pools. One afternoon I found an old man kneeling beside her creation, arranging snail shells. He looked up, embarrassed, said, “My grandson used to do this.” Then he placed a leaf like a trampoline and walked away. I never saw him again. Maybe he was a memory I hadn’t wrote down yet.

I read somewhere that the average person lives about 27,375 days. I did the math while waiting for my coffee to brew: I’m past the halfway point. The realization sat in my stomach like swallowed ice. Not because I fear death—though I do, every time I fly, picturing the oxygen masks dropping like jellyfish—but because of the sheer velocity. How did I use up so many days brushing teeth, re-heating leftovers, looking for my keys? The Buddhists say we die each moment and are reborn; maybe that’s why the days feel both long and short, like a dream where you’re running but the hallway extends. I think of the river again, how it was never the same water, never the same me dipping toes in to test for leeches. Yet something persists: the shape of motion, the way sunlight at 5 p.m. in October turns every leaf into stained glass. Maybe that’s what we mean by soul—not a thing but a pattern, music you can still hum after the radio’s been thrown out.

Last winter I went back to the old house. The current owners let me walk around, though they clearly thought I was casing the joint for burglary. The maple we planted for Earth Day 1992 now towers over the roof, roots buckling the driveway like slow-motion earthquakes. The creek—my creek—was frozen, a ribbon of dull quartz. I stepped onto it carefully, testing. Halfway across, I lay down, ear to the ice, and listened. Somewhere beneath, water still moved, a low growl like distant traffic. I thought about the journals in my mother’s attic, the girl with the eyebrow ring, the woman on the bus who tasted like chlorine. All of us versions of the same river, pretending we’re not heading toward the same vast, anonymous sea. A crow landed on a branch, cocked its head. I said, “I know, I know,” though I didn’t know anything, not really. The sky began to snow, small dry flakes like fallout. I stood up, brushed off, walked back to the rental car smelling of winterized earth. Behind me, the creek kept arguing with itself, patient, implacable, already forgetting I’d ever been there.

Driving away, I passed the elementary school where we once released balloons with notes attached—our addresses in careful ballpoint, asking whoever found them to write back. I never got a reply. But last year a woman in Manitoba emailed me through Facebook: she’d found a deflated balloon in her horse’s water trough, the note bleached to ghosts. She typed: “I just wanted you to know it traveled 1,200 miles. I was eight once too.” We corresponded for a week, then lost touch, the way you do with people who know too much about your past. Still, I picture her standing in a field, holding a piece of my sky, and I feel the river loop back on itself, a Möbius strip of longing.

So much of adulthood is maintenance: replacing filters, scheduling dental cleanings, Googling whether the mold in the shower is the kind that kills you or just ruins grout. We carve out islands of meaning—birthday cakes shaped like dinosaurs, front-porch tomatoes that actually taste like summer, the way my daughter still mispronounces “animal” as “aminal,” though I’ll correct her one day and instantly regret it. These islands shrink if you stare too long. Better to keep moving, to trust that like Water—so insistent it can carve grand canyons—our small collections of days will, in aggregate, sculpt something that looks, from far away, like purpose.

I’ve stopped keeping a journal, but I write notes on my phone at 2 a.m.: “She said stars are the holes in the blanket of the sky where heaven peeks through.” “The sound of snow falling is actually the sound of your own blood.” “Remember the way grief tastes like pennies?” The next morning they read like drunk text messages from my better self. I never delete them. One day I’ll have to switch phones, and they’ll vanish into the same landfill where old balloons go, but for now they feel like breadcrumbs, a way to find my way back to whatever it is I’m trying to say.

Sometimes I think the only real story is this: we are here, and then we are not. The river knows it; that’s why it keeps moving, not out of urgency but courtesy. It has seen trilobites, steam engines, my grandfather’s discharge papers, the way my daughter’s hand feels in mine—warm, sticky, impossibly small. It will see the malls crumble, the last bee, the final light switched off. And still it will argue over rocks, smooth them into sentences we haven’t learned to read yet. We call that erosion, but maybe it’s just another word for tenderness—how the world, in time, polishes even our sharpest edges into something that can fit in a pocket, be skipped across the surface, sink with a quiet plink that sounds, if you’re listening, like the period at the end of a very long sentence that never really ends, only yields to the next clause, the next voice, the next small brave craft of sticks and string we launch in the dark, hoping—against all evidence—that this time the current will carry it somewhere we were always meant to go.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
